metadata.file_name: 88. Park et al. (2023). Understanding students’ problem-solving patterns: Evidence from an allotted response time in a PISA 2012 item.pdf
metadata.title: Understanding students’ problem-solving patterns: Evidence from an allotted response time in a PISA 2012 item

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study analyzed PISA 2012 computer-based assessment (CPS) log data for the Ticket item (CP038q1) from 42 countries, focusing on students who received full credit. The manuscript reports the analytic sample sizes and log-data cleaning (e.g., excluding cases without START_ITEM/END_ITEM) but does not mention applying PISA sampling weights or any complex survey design adjustments. Because the analyses are based on Elastic Net variable selection and k-medoids clustering on process-time variables (and the paper does not describe weight-aware estimation), survey weights are treated as not applied. No replicate-weight method (e.g., BRR/jackknife) is reported.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The dependent variables in this study are derived from CPS process logs (time spent on item steps and action counts) and the clustering outcomes are problem-solving patterns, not PV-based cognitive achievement scores. Although the authors exclude a small number of cases lacking START_ITEM/END_ITEM markers, they do not describe a general missing-data strategy for other variables (e.g., how missing timestamps or derived time variables were handled). Therefore, missing-data handling is not reported in a way that maps to standard PV/missing-data procedures.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA 2012 CPS log data for the Ticket item CP038q1 (Vending Machine) from 42 countries. For the main analyses (RQ1/RQ2), the study restricted to students who provided the correct answer (full credit) and further excluded 4 students who lacked a start (“START_ITEM”) or end (“END_ITEM”) marker in the raw log data, leaving 7,191 students for variable generation and clustering. For RQ3, the same variable-generation and selection/clustering pipeline was applied to students with incorrect answers (partial/incorrect credit), using the corresponding cleaned log traces.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 CPS (Problem Solving) — Ticket item CP038q1 (Vending Machine), correct-answer subset
main_findings.performance_metrics: Elastic Net variable selection (10-fold CV on training set; repeated 100 times). Test-set RMSE: 0.18.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 CPS Ticket item CP038q1 data for students with the correct answer, the study leveraged time_start, time_b1, time_b3, time_irrelevant, and time_avg_btw_events to predict overall problem-solving ability (PISA scale-score ratio), finding that these time-related process variables were the most consistently selected and yielded a test-set RMSE of 0.18. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study used PISA 2012 CPS log data for the Ticket item (CP038q1) and focused on students with full credit to identify problem-solving patterns from allotted response-time variables. For RQ1, Elastic Net variable selection (10-fold cross-validation, repeated 100 times) selected five time-related predictors (time_b1, time_b3, time_start, time_irrelevant, time_avg_btw_events) and achieved a test-set RMSE of 0.18 when predicting overall problem-solving ability operationalized as a PISA scale-score ratio. For RQ2, students were clustered into four k-medoids groups (curious, speedy, repetitive, strategic) based on the selected time variables, and the clusters differed in exploration vs. decision-making timing and action patterns as visualized by process maps. For RQ3, a similar Elastic Net + clustering approach on incorrect-answer students produced eight clusters that could be roughly mapped to the four correct-answer patterns. The paper does not report survey-weight usage and does not provide a general missing-data handling protocol beyond excluding cases without start/end markers.
